slides.title: Nationalisms: South Africa, the Middle East and Africa
slides.lead: Comparing four nationalist movements - Afrikaner and African nationalism in South Africa, and Zionism and Arab nationalism in the Middle East - to ask how nationalism can both unite a people and divide a land.

media.alt: Four stylised flags radiating from a central globe, representing four nationalist movements being compared.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='160' cy='90' r='34' fill='#7a8b5a'/><circle cx='160' cy='90' r='34' fill='none' stroke='#4f5d3a' stroke-width='2'/><g stroke='#5a4a2a' stroke-width='3'><line x1='160' y1='90' x2='60' y2='34'/><line x1='160' y1='90' x2='262' y2='34'/><line x1='160' y1='90' x2='60' y2='150'/><line x1='160' y1='90' x2='262' y2='150'/></g><rect x='40' y='22' width='34' height='22' fill='#e8a13a'/><rect x='246' y='22' width='34' height='22' fill='#1f3a8a'/><rect x='40' y='140' width='34' height='22' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='246' y='140' width='34' height='22' fill='#2f5233'/></svg>

slides.notes: Frame the whole unit as a comparative study, not four separate stories. State the central historical question: why does nationalism - a force that gives people identity, pride and freedom - so often produce exclusion and conflict? Preview the structure: first define nationalism, then examine the two South African cases (Afrikaner and African), then the two Middle Eastern cases (Zionism and Arab nationalism), comparing as we go. Remind learners that some of this history is deeply contested and still emotional today, so we handle sources critically and respectfully.

slides.title: What is nationalism?
slides.lead: Nationalism is the belief that a group of people who share an identity form a 'nation' that deserves its own state, loyalty and self-determination.

blocks.text: A **nation** is an *imagined community*: its members will never meet most of their fellow citizens, yet they feel they belong together. **Nationalism** turns that feeling into a political demand - the right to rule themselves in a state of their own.

Historians often distinguish two types. **Civic nationalism** defines the nation by shared citizenship, laws and values, so anyone can join. **Ethnic nationalism** defines it by shared descent, language, religion or culture, which can exclude outsiders. Most real movements mix the two.

items: It usually rests on a shared language, religion, history or sense of common ancestry.
It is often sparked or hardened by a shared grievance - defeat, poverty, discrimination or foreign rule.
It builds unity by defining an 'us', but in doing so it also defines a 'them'.

slides.notes: Give learners the analytical vocabulary they will use for the rest of the unit: nation, nationalism, self-determination, civic vs ethnic nationalism, and Benedict Anderson's idea of the 'imagined community'. Stress that nationalism is neither simply good nor simply bad - it drove anti-colonial liberation and it drove exclusion and apartheid. The skill being taught is comparison: same concept, different contexts.

slides.title: Five questions to ask of any nationalism
slides.lead: Use the same lens on each movement so you can compare them fairly.

blocks.heading: Who?
blocks.text: Who counts as part of the nation - and who is left out?

blocks.heading: Why now?
blocks.text: What grievance or crisis triggered the movement?

blocks.heading: How?
blocks.text: How did leaders mobilise people - culture, religion, organisations, mass action?

blocks.heading: Land?
blocks.text: What territory did they claim, and who else claimed it?

blocks.heading: Result?
blocks.text: Did it bring liberation, domination, conflict - or all three?

slides.notes: This is the comparative framework learners should apply to Afrikaner nationalism, African nationalism, Zionism and Arab nationalism in turn. Encourage them to keep a comparison grid in their books with these five columns. Point out that the 'Land?' question is where the two case studies become tragic: in both South Africa and Israel/Palestine, two nationalisms claimed the same territory.

slides.title: Afrikaner nationalism: defeat, poverty and revival
slides.lead: Afrikaner nationalism grew out of humiliation in the South African War (1899-1902) and the poverty many Afrikaners faced afterwards.

blocks.text: Defeat by Britain, the death of women and children in concentration camps, and the loss of the Boer republics left many Afrikaners feeling that their volk (people) was under threat. In the following decades tens of thousands became 'poor whites', pushed off the land and into the cities.

Leaders used **language, religion and history** to rebuild pride. Afrikaans was standardised and promoted; the Dutch Reformed Church stressed the Afrikaners as a chosen people; and the story of the Great Trek became a founding myth of a people struggling for freedom.

items: The **Afrikaner Broederbond** (founded 1918) built a secret network to advance Afrikaner interests in politics, business and culture.
Cultural bodies and the 1938 centenary of the Great Trek stirred mass emotion.
The **National Party** channelled this identity into a bid for state power.

media.alt: An ox-wagon silhouette below a rising sun, evoking the Great Trek used as a nationalist symbol.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='170' cy='58' r='26' fill='#e8a13a'/><rect y='118' width='240' height='42' fill='#c8b89a'/><g fill='#5a4a2a'><rect x='60' y='92' width='70' height='24'/><path d='M60 92 l14 -16 h44 l12 16 Z'/></g><circle cx='74' cy='120' r='12' fill='none' stroke='#5a4a2a' stroke-width='4'/><circle cx='120' cy='120' r='12' fill='none' stroke='#5a4a2a' stroke-width='4'/><line x1='130' y1='104' x2='168' y2='100' stroke='#5a4a2a' stroke-width='3'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain how a sense of victimhood was turned into a political programme. Key mechanism: grievance (defeat, poor-white poverty) plus cultural mobilisation (language, church, historical myth, the Broederbond) equals a mass movement. The 1938 Great Trek centenary is a good example of manufactured tradition. [SME] Handle the concentration-camp memory carefully and accurately; it is historically real and was later used politically. Flag that this is ethnic nationalism defined by descent, language and religion.

slides.title: 1948: Afrikaner nationalism captures the state
slides.lead: In 1948 the National Party won power and turned Afrikaner nationalism into the official policy of apartheid.

blocks.text: Led by **D.F. Malan**, the National Party won the whites-only 1948 election on the promise of *apartheid* ('apartness'). Afrikaner nationalism now controlled the state and used it to protect Afrikaner jobs, language and power - and to enforce white domination over the black majority.

items: The state expanded Afrikaans schooling, the civil service and state-owned industry to uplift Afrikaners.
Apartheid laws classified people by race and reserved power and privilege for whites.
This is a case of a nationalism that achieved self-determination for one group by denying it to others.

media.alt: A rising bar chart in front of a government building, showing one group gaining power at others' expense.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='30' y='36' width='120' height='40' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M54 36 q36 -22 72 0 Z' fill='#b7a98d'/><rect x='42' y='48' width='8' height='28' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='130' y='48' width='8' height='28' fill='#f4efe6'/><g fill='#2f5233'><rect x='160' y='108' width='16' height='28'/><rect x='182' y='88' width='16' height='48'/><rect x='204' y='60' width='16' height='76'/></g><rect x='28' y='136' width='196' height='6' fill='#5a4a2a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Draw the analytical point sharply: Afrikaner nationalism 'succeeded' in 1948, but its success was other people's oppression. This sets up the direct clash with African nationalism. Connect back to the framework: Who was included? (Afrikaners, then whites.) Who was excluded? (The black majority.) Note that apartheid was partly a job-creation and upliftment project for poor Afrikaners, which helps explain its appeal to its own community.

slides.title: African nationalism: unity against exclusion
slides.lead: African nationalism grew in response to conquest, land loss and exclusion from the Union of South Africa formed in 1910.

blocks.text: The Union of South Africa (1910) united whites but shut Africans out of political power. In 1912 educated African leaders founded the **South African Native National Congress** - renamed the **African National Congress (ANC)** in 1923 - to unite Africans across ethnic and regional lines and to demand rights in one common nation.

The **1913 Natives Land Act**, which reserved most of the country's land for whites, showed why unity was urgent. Early African nationalism was moderate: it used petitions, deputations and the law to appeal for inclusion.

items: It aimed to overcome ethnic divisions and build a single African, and later non-racial, South African nation.
It defined the nation by shared citizenship and rights - a more civic vision.
Its early methods were constitutional; frustration would later drive it towards mass action.

media.alt: Many small figures of different shapes joining hands to form one circle, symbolising unity across groups.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><g fill='#2f5233'><circle cx='120' cy='40' r='11'/><circle cx='176' cy='62' r='11'/><circle cx='176' cy='108' r='11'/><circle cx='120' cy='130' r='11'/><circle cx='64' cy='108' r='11'/><circle cx='64' cy='62' r='11'/></g><circle cx='120' cy='85' r='52' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='4' stroke-dasharray='6 6'/></svg>

slides.notes: Contrast the founding logic with Afrikaner nationalism: African nationalism sought to build unity across many African ethnic groups (Zulu, Xhosa, Sotho and others) precisely to resist a state that excluded them. Emphasise the more civic, inclusive vision - the ANC ultimately moved towards non-racialism. Note the irony the unit keeps returning to: two South African nationalisms, one demanding domination, one demanding inclusion, colliding over the same land.

slides.title: A new generation: the ANC Youth League
slides.lead: By the 1940s a younger generation demanded a bolder, mass-based African nationalism.

blocks.text: Frustrated by decades of polite petitions, young leaders formed the **ANC Youth League** in 1944. Its first president, **Anton Lembede**, argued that Africans must free themselves through pride, self-reliance and mass organisation rather than waiting for white goodwill.
Africa is a black man's country... Africans are one. Out of the heterogeneous tribes there must emerge a homogeneous nation.
blocks.cite: Anton Lembede, 'Some Basic Principles of African Nationalism', 1945

items: The Youth League's 1949 Programme of Action embraced strikes, boycotts and civil disobedience.
Figures such as Nelson Mandela, Walter Sisulu and Oliver Tambo rose through its ranks.
This assertive African nationalism would drive the mass struggle against apartheid in the 1950s.

slides.notes: Use the Lembede quotation to show the emotional core of African nationalism - self-reliance and unity across ethnic lines - while noting that the ANC's mainstream tradition also moved towards non-racialism (later the Freedom Charter). [SME] Lembede's Africanism was one strand; the ANC contained a debate between exclusive Africanism and inclusive non-racialism, which later produced the PAC breakaway (1959). Keep the nuance: African nationalism was not monolithic. Link forward: this generation leads the Defiance Campaign that Grade 9 covered.

slides.title: Two nationalisms, one country
slides.lead: Afrikaner and African nationalism grew side by side, each shaping the other.

items: Rooted in defeat by Britain and poor-white poverty.
Ethnic: defined by descent, Afrikaans and the church.
Goal: self-rule and protection for the Afrikaner volk.
Won the state in 1948 and built apartheid.
Achieved freedom for itself by dominating others.
Rooted in conquest, land loss and exclusion.
Civic aim: unite many groups into one nation.
Goal: equal rights and majority self-rule.
Radicalised into mass struggle from the 1940s-50s.
Sought freedom through inclusion, not domination.

slides.notes: Pause here for a structured comparison exercise. Ask learners to identify similarities (both used history, culture and organisation to mobilise; both demanded self-determination) and differences (one exclusive and ruling, one inclusive and excluded). Stress the interaction: Afrikaner nationalist repression radicalised African nationalism, and African resistance hardened Afrikaner nationalism. This mutual shaping is a key higher-order insight for FET.

slides.title: Zionism: a homeland for the Jewish people
slides.lead: Zionism was the nationalist movement calling for a Jewish homeland, largely in response to centuries of persecution in Europe.

blocks.text: Faced with violent anti-Semitism - pogroms in the Russian Empire and the Dreyfus Affair in France - **Theodor Herzl** argued in *The Jewish State* (1896) that Jews would only be safe with a state of their own. The First Zionist Congress met at Basel in 1897, and Jewish migration to Palestine grew.

In the **Balfour Declaration (1917)** Britain announced support for 'a national home for the Jewish people' in Palestine - a land that was then overwhelmingly Arab. Britain ruled Palestine under a League of Nations mandate after 1920, and tensions between Jewish and Arab communities rose.

items: Zionism blended ethnic and religious identity with a modern demand for statehood.
It was driven by real and deadly persecution, above all the Holocaust of 1941-1945.
But the land it claimed was already home to an Arab population with its own national feeling.

media.alt: A six-pointed star above a stylised horizon, representing the Zionist call for a Jewish homeland.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='118' width='240' height='42' fill='#c8b89a'/><g fill='none' stroke='#1f3a8a' stroke-width='6' stroke-linejoin='round'><path d='M120 34 L146 94 L86 58 L154 58 L94 94 Z'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Present Zionism as a response to genuine, murderous persecution, culminating in the Holocaust - learners must understand why so many Jews concluded they needed their own state. At the same time, be factually clear that Palestine was already inhabited, mostly by Arabs, so the same land carried two claims. [SME] This is highly sensitive contested history; keep to widely agreed facts (Herzl, Basel 1897, Balfour 1917, British Mandate) and avoid taking sides. Model source criticism: the Balfour Declaration also promised not to prejudice the rights of 'existing non-Jewish communities'.

slides.title: 1948: independence and catastrophe
slides.lead: The same event of 1948 is remembered by two peoples in opposite ways.

blocks.text: After the Holocaust and rising conflict, the United Nations voted in 1947 to partition Palestine into a Jewish and an Arab state. On **14 May 1948** Jewish leaders declared the **State of Israel**. War with neighbouring Arab states followed at once.

Israelis remember 1948 as their **War of Independence** - the achievement of a safe homeland. Palestinians remember it as the **Nakba** ('catastrophe'): an estimated 700 000 Palestinian Arabs fled or were expelled and were not allowed to return. One land, two nations, two irreconcilable memories.

items: For one nation, 1948 meant self-determination and survival.
For the other, it meant dispossession and exile.
This unresolved clash still shapes the Middle East today.

media.alt: A single territory split by a vertical line, one side rising and one side departing, symbolising two opposite memories of 1948.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='16' y='30' width='96' height='100' fill='#a8bcae'/><rect x='128' y='30' width='96' height='100' fill='#d8c6a8'/><rect x='116' y='24' width='8' height='112' fill='#8a1f1f'/><g fill='#1f3a8a'><path d='M64 60 L74 84 L54 70 L86 70 L66 84 Z'/></g><g fill='#5a4a2a'><circle cx='160' cy='72' r='7'/><rect x='153' y='80' width='14' height='24'/><path d='M172 88 h22 l-8 -8 v16 Z'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: This is the most sensitive slide in the deck; teach it as a lesson in perspective and historical memory. Present both names for 1948 side by side without ranking them, and use the language of 'remembered as'. [SME] Numbers and causes of the Palestinian exodus are genuinely contested by historians (flight vs expulsion); present the ~700,000 figure as an estimate and note the debate. The teaching aim is empathy and source criticism: the same dated event produces two national narratives. Invite calm, respectful discussion and set ground rules first.

slides.title: Arab nationalism: unity and independence
slides.lead: Arab nationalism sought to free Arabic-speaking peoples from foreign rule and, for some, to unite them into one nation.

blocks.text: As the Ottoman Empire collapsed after World War I, Arab lands were divided into British and French mandates. Arab nationalists resented this foreign control and the borders drawn by Europeans, and increasingly opposed Zionist settlement in Palestine.

The movement peaked under **Gamal Abdel Nasser** of Egypt. When Nasser **nationalised the Suez Canal in 1956** and faced down a British, French and Israeli invasion, he became a hero across the Arab world and a symbol for anti-colonial movements throughout Africa and Asia.

items: It was built on a shared Arabic language, culture and (for many) Islam.
**Pan-Arabism** dreamed of uniting all Arabs, briefly joining Egypt and Syria in 1958.
It was strongly anti-colonial and inspired African independence struggles.

media.alt: A broken chain above a stylised canal, representing anti-colonial Arab nationalism and the Suez Crisis.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='104' y='30' width='32' height='108' fill='#4f7fae'/><g fill='none' stroke='#2f5233' stroke-width='7'><circle cx='72' cy='58' r='14'/><circle cx='96' cy='72' r='14'/></g><g fill='none' stroke='#2f5233' stroke-width='7'><circle cx='168' cy='58' r='14'/><circle cx='144' cy='72' r='14'/></g><line x1='108' y1='66' x2='132' y2='66' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='6' stroke-dasharray='4 5'/></svg>

slides.notes: Show Arab nationalism as another anti-colonial movement, which links the Middle East case back to Africa (the CAPS topic spans both). Nasser and Suez 1956 are the set-piece: a smaller power defying former colonial masters electrified the decolonising world, including sub-Saharan Africa. Note the tension within Arab nationalism between pan-Arab unity and separate nation-states, and that opposition to Zionism was one of its unifying causes. Connect the anti-colonial energy to African nationalism studied earlier.

slides.title: Two nationalisms, one land
slides.lead: Like South Africa, the Middle East saw two nationalisms claim the same territory.

items: Response to European anti-Semitism and the Holocaust.
Ethnic-religious: a homeland for the Jewish people.
Goal: a secure Jewish state in Palestine.
Achieved statehood in 1948 (Israel).
Its success displaced the Palestinian Arab population.
Response to Ottoman collapse and European colonial rule.
Based on shared Arabic language and culture.
Goal: independence and (for some) Arab unity.
Peaked with Nasser and the Suez Crisis of 1956.
Opposed Zionism and inspired anti-colonial Africa.

slides.notes: Run the same comparison drill as the South African slide. Then make the cross-case link explicit: both South Africa and Palestine show two nationalisms colliding over one territory, producing long, painful conflict. Ask learners to compare not just Zionism vs Arab nationalism, but Afrikaner nationalism vs Zionism (both minorities seeking security and dominance) and African nationalism vs Arab nationalism (both anti-colonial mass movements). This four-way comparison is exactly the higher-order thinking CAPS rewards.

slides.title: Nationalism: a force that unites and divides
slides.lead: Across all four cases, nationalism gave people identity and freedom - and drew lines that led to conflict.

blocks.alt: Timeline of key nationalist milestones from 1897 to 1958 across South Africa and the Middle East.

events.label: SANNC (later ANC) founded in South Africa
Balfour Declaration on a Jewish 'national home'
ANC Youth League radicalises African nationalism
National Party wins power; State of Israel declared / the Nakba
Nasser and the Suez Crisis: peak of Arab nationalism

slides.notes: Consolidate with this shared timeline so learners see the four movements unfolding in the same era, influencing one another (anti-colonialism spread ideas across borders). Draw the concluding argument: nationalism is a double-edged force - it powered liberation from colonialism and apartheid, yet where two nations claimed one land it produced dispossession and lasting conflict. Note 1948 as an extraordinary hinge year appearing in two different case studies at once.

slides.title: Review: comparing the nationalisms
slides.lead: Use the five-question framework and the comparisons to answer the questions below.

blocks.text: In this lesson we defined nationalism, distinguished **civic** from **ethnic** nationalism, and compared four movements: **Afrikaner** and **African** nationalism in South Africa, and **Zionism** and **Arab** nationalism in the Middle East. In both regions two nationalisms claimed the same land, with far-reaching consequences.

blocks.question: Explain the difference between civic and ethnic nationalism, using ONE South African and ONE Middle Eastern example.
Compare the origins of Afrikaner nationalism and African nationalism. Refer to at least two similarities and two differences.

'The events of 1948 meant freedom for one nation and loss for another.' Using either the South African OR the Israeli-Palestinian example, discuss how the same historical moment can be remembered in opposite ways.

slides.notes: Use the three questions as written work or a structured class debate; the mark allocations mirror CAPS extended-writing and source-based styles. Question 3 explicitly assesses the skill of understanding contested memory and perspective, which is central to this topic. Remind learners to argue from evidence and to represent each side fairly. [SME] Before assessing the Israel/Palestine material, ensure the department's guidance on teaching this contested topic has been followed and that a range of sources is provided.
